title: Sounding right
intro: Azerbaijani has a music to it — some parts are said loud, some almost vanish. Listen to these greetings first, then learn the rules behind them.

theory.heading: Lean on the end
theory.body:
Azerbaijani usually stresses the LAST syllable — say it a touch louder and longer.
• salam → sa-LAM
• yaxşı → yax-ŞI
Fight the English habit of hitting the first syllable.

theory.heading: Quiet little endings
theory.body:
Small grammar endings stay soft, so the loud part is the syllable just before them.
• Necəsən? → nə-CƏ-sən (not necə-SƏN)
• the question -mı, the negative -ma, and the 'I am / you are' endings (-am, -san) all stay quiet.

theory.heading: ğ is a ghost
theory.body:
The letter ğ is never a hard 'g'. After back vowels (a, ı, o, u) it almost disappears and just stretches the vowel before it.
• dağ ≈ 'daa'
• sağ ol ≈ 'saa ol'
Near front vowels it softens toward a light 'y'.

theory.heading: Never swallow ə
theory.body: ə is a clear, bright vowel like the 'a' in 'cat'. Say it fully every time, loud or quiet — don't let it melt into a lazy English 'uh'.

title: The verb comes last
intro: Word order is where Azerbaijani feels different from English. Hear these, then see the pattern.

theory.heading: Subject – Object – Verb
theory.body:
The verb goes at the END.
• Səni sevirəm = literally 'you I-love' (English puts 'love' in the middle).
• Su istəyirəm = 'water I-want'.
Name the thing first, put the action last.

theory.heading: Drop the 'I'
theory.body:
The verb ending already says who, so you can drop the pronoun.
• İstəyirəm = 'I want' (no need for 'mən').
• Sevirəm = 'I love'.
The -əm ending means 'I'.

theory.heading: Everything leads to the verb
theory.body:
Extra words stack up before the verb:
• Səninlə danışmaq istəyirəm = 'with-you to-talk I-want'.
Whatever you add, the verb still lands at the very end.

title: Why the endings change
intro: Ever notice the endings shift between -ım, -im, -um, -üm? There's a simple rule. Listen, then learn it.

theory.heading: Vowel harmony
theory.body:
Endings copy the vowel 'colour' of the word.
• Back vowels a, ı, o, u → back endings.
• Front vowels e, ə, i, ö, ü → front endings.
That's why one ending shows up as -ım / -im / -um / -üm.

theory.heading: 'My' changes shape
theory.body:
Same meaning, vowel matched to the word:
• ata → atam (my dad)
• ev → evim (my home)
• pul → pulum (my money)

theory.heading: Hard at the end, soft inside
theory.body: A final b/d/g/c hardens: kitab sounds like 'kitap'. But add a vowel ending and the soft sound comes back: kitab → kitabım.

title: Now, and asking
intro: How to say what you're doing, and how to ask. Hear the question words first.

theory.heading: The -ir present
theory.body:
Add -ır / -ir / -ur / -ür + a person ending for 'I'm —ing':
• istə → istəyirəm (I want)
• gəl → gəlirəm (I'm coming)
The suffix vowel follows harmony.

theory.heading: Yes / no questions
theory.body:
Two easy ways:
• Add the soft particle -mı/-mi/-mu/-mü: Gəlirsən → Gəlirsənmi?
• Or just raise your voice at the end: Yaxşısan? ('You're good?').
Keep the particle quiet — don't stress it.

theory.heading: Question words
theory.body:
nə (what), kim (who), harada (where), nə vaxt (when), niyə (why), necə (how). They sit where the missing info would go:
• Harada yaşayırsan? = 'Where do you live?'

title: Everyday phrases
intro: A pack of phrases you'll actually use with Dad. Listen and say each one out loud — whole phrases stick better than single words.

theory.heading: Practice tip
theory.body: Don't translate word-by-word — learn each phrase as one chunk, with its melody. Say it out loud right after the audio, copying the loud and quiet parts. These are all in your Review now, too.
